[[RunningHead]]

Does the Use of the Couch Facilitate the Analytic Process? An Empirical Investigation

Katie Doorn DClinPsy , Samantha Mahr PsyD , Sarah Horne MSc , Bernard Gorman PHD , Karl Stukenberg PHD and Sherwood Waldron MD

Sherwood Waldron

567 West Yangsi Road, Pudong, Shanghai 200126, China
Email: kav9239@nyu.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2584-5897

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2136-1695

New York, New York, United States

Sherwood Waldron  

analytic process, psychoanalytic treatment, couch, chair

The use of the couch has been thought to be integral to the facilitation of a psychoanalytic process and an essential component of the therapeutic frame. Yet, the observed therapeutic differences between sessions conducted on the chair and on the couch have not been adequately examined. Thus, we aimed to compare differences in observed therapeutic process (patient behavior, analyst behavior, and their interaction in sessions) between sessions in which the patient lies on the couch and sessions in which the patient sits on a chair. We also examined if the patient's position (couch vs. chair) predicts the extent to which the therapeutic process reflects an ideal psychoanalytic session. Based on the observer codings on the Psychotherapy Process Q Set (Jones, 2000) in a sample of 287 sessions from 27 psychoanalytic treatments, we compared the most and least characteristic aspects of the therapeutic process in the 197 sessions conducted on the couch and the 90 sessions conducted on the chair. We then compared the level of resemblance to the expert prototype of the ideal analytic process (Psychoanalytic Prototype of the Process Q Set; Ablon & Jones, 2005). The analytic process in chair and couch sessions largely overlapped. We did not detect significant differences in the analytic process between sessions conducted on the couch or chair nor differences in resemblance to the Psychoanalytic Prototype of the Process Q Set prototype. A more flexible approach to analytic work would be supported in the light of our findings. There may be a variety of ways in which either the couch or chair may facilitate analytic work.

Commentary: Improving Clinical Case Formulation in Developmental Psychopathology: A Case Commentary on Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (PDM-2) in a Multimethod Assessment Framework

Francesca Locati PHD , Luca Corti MPsyc , Robert Gordon PHD and Emanuela Brusadelli PHD

Emanuela Brusadelli

Piazza Botta 6, 27100 Pavia, Italy
Email: francesca.locati@unipv.it
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9720-009X

Milano, Italy

Allentown, Pennsylvania, United States

Emanuela Brusadelli  

Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual–2, adolescence, psychological assessment, testing

The Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual–2 challenges the constraints of traditional nosographic frameworks by offering clinicians a comprehensive guide for case formulation. This article aimed to underscore the significance of these features within the sensitive context of developmental psychopathology. The Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual–2's capacity to not only provide a nuanced clinical perspective that captures an individual's functioning in a holistic and integrative manner but also to guide the development of personalized treatment plans is particularly emphasized. Through a clinical case study, this article aims to discuss assessment methodologies. Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual–2 compares data derived from psychometric testing with insights from clinical observation while contextualizing these findings within the patient's specific developmental phase.

[[RunningHead]]

Theaters of the Skin: The Representation of Emotional Dramas Among Female Patients Engaging in Skin Cutting

Anita Moe PHD , Eric Stänicke PHD and Hanne Haavind PHD

Hanne Haavind

Forskningsveien 3A, 0373 Oslo, Norway
Email: anitam@uio.no

Hanne Haavind  

self-harm, skin cutting, trauma, shame

Female patients in mental health care services with extensive experiences of deliberate self-harm were invited to personal interviews about what was happening in their body and in their mind when they cut their skin. A series of 12 cases revealed the symbolic potential of skin cutting as a “staged” event, where each step—from cutting and bleeding to the formation of the wound, scab, and scar—represented emotional and embodied experiences that could not otherwise be expressed. In this article, we have explored this potential and the qualities the skin offered to the women who severely harmed themselves. We identified three main themes in the personal histories of these women: Their self-harming activities had a source in their personal histories of being exposed to boundary violations; cutting was an activity that gave them an experience of a “leaking” self; and cutting represented intense and unbearable emotions. The findings are discussed in the context of Joyce McDougall's use of the theater as a metaphor for psychic reality as well as the psychoanalytic theories of trauma and second skin formation. Finally, we argue that psychological dramas related to shame may fit the “skin stage” particularly well.

[[RunningHead]]

The Relationship between Attachment, the Capacity to Love, and Relationship Satisfaction: A Mediation Analysis

Jürgen Fuchshuber PHD , Johanna Neugebauer MD , Victor Blüml MD PHD and Nestor Kapusta MD PHD

Nestor Kapusta

Währinger Gürtel 18–20, 1090 Vienna, Austria
Email: juergen.fuchshuber@meduniwien.ac.at
https://orcid.org/0009-0003-2932-9029

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-0226-0837

Vienna, Austria
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0970-4341

Nestor Kapusta  

attachment, capacity to love, relationship satisfaction, path analysis, mediation

Previous research has repeatedly highlighted the strong connection between attachment patterns and the quality of intimate relationships. The present study will investigate Kernberg's concept of the capacity to love (CTL) as a possible mediator of the link between attachment and relationship quality. A total sample of 782 participants currently in a relationship (88% female; Mage = 29 years, SD = 8.87; range = 17–75) was investigated. Mediational analysis was conducted via path analysis to examine the relationship between adult attachment, the CTL, and relationship satisfaction (RS). The results suggest that significant negative effects of both attachment avoidance and anxiety mediated via specific facets of CTL onto RS. Attachment avoidance was fully mediated, while attachment anxiety was partially mediated. Overall, attachment and CTL scales were able to explain 57% of the variance of satisfaction in close relationships total score, while attachment explained between 10% and 52% of CTL scales variance. The findings indicate that several dimensions of CTL are important hallmarks of RS mediating especially the adverse effects of an avoidant attachment attitude on RS. These findings might prove as important starting point for the psychotherapeutic treatment of intimate interpersonal problems.

Subjective Experience: Its Fate in Psychology, Psychoanalysis and Philosophy of Mind, by Morris N. Eagle. New York: Routledge, 2024. 262 pp., $42.99 (ISBN 9781032686950).

Roger Frie PHD PsyD RPsych

Roger Frie

Vancouver, BC V6B 5K3, Canada
Email: roger.frie@sfu.ca

Roger Frie  

Thirty years ago, I had the good fortune to be taught a course on Freud by Morris Eagle. He was the Roberta Held-Weiss scholar that year at the William Alanson White Institute of Psychiatry, Psychoanalysis and Psychology in New York, and I was a first-year analytic candidate. I was not new to Freud, having come to psychoanalytic training by way of being an academic in the humanities. I had sat through more than a few doctoral courses and postdoctoral seminars in which Freud's work was discussed, dissected, and deconstructed. But nothing I had experienced up until that point could compare with the clarity of Eagle's thinking or the seamless and persuasive narratives that he wove to explain the origins and nature of Freud's thought. It was a delight. As I listened, I made copious notes, probably more than in all of my other analytic courses combined. Those notes have accompanied me ever since through several decades of teaching psychology to undergraduate, graduate, and postgraduate students. My “Morris Eagle notes,” as I refer to them, are not only my fall back but they form the foundation for how I have come to understand and teach Freud.

I am taking the liberty of sharing this personal anecdote because it is directly relevant to the task at hand: reviewing Eagle's new book, Subjective Experience: Its Fate in Psychology, Psycho-analysis and Philosophy of Mind. I can think of few people I would rather follow through the thicket of ideas that comprise this book-length series of reflections about past and present theories of mind. And indeed, what stands out about this work is not only the clarity of Eagle's analyses but his facility with ideas across the three different yet interrelated disciplines that he examines. There is much to learn, and we are lucky to have a master instructor to teach us.

But this recognition also creates a conundrum of sorts. Whatever I say will pale in comparison with the analyses that Eagle offers. So rather than leave my concluding remarks about Subjective Experience: Its Fate in Psychology, Psychoanalysis and Philosophy of Mind to the end of my review, let me begin by stating simply: This book is not only worth reading but it is the kind of work that will reward the reader time and again. In short, if you want to understand the role and nature of subjective experience across psychology, psychoanalysis, and philosophy, I can think of few better places to turn. To be sure, Subjective Experience is not a quick or easy read. But nor should it be. There is a tremendous amount to be learned and much to savor.

Michael O'Loughlin  

Carolyn Laubender's focus is the clinic as a political site. This book explores the work of six 20th-century analysts, Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, Wulf Sachs, D. W. Winnicott, Thomas Main, and John Bowlby, examining concepts such as “authority, reparation, anticolonialism, mothering, communalism, and security” (p. 3) to illustrate how politics and ideology undergird the practice of each analyst. She states her purpose thus:

I therefore consider how psychoanalysts become formidable political actors precisely through their clinical work, where they implicitly transformed the privatized space of the clinic into a prototypical laboratory for reimagining formations of race, gender, sexuality, childhood, nation, and democracy. By deliberately examining clinicians and techniques that represent some of the most conservative and orthodox traditions of psychoanalytic practice, part of this book's claim is that even the most “classical” psychoanalytic clinics perform implicit (and often explicit) political work. (p. 3)

Using postcolonial, feminist, and queer theories to examine the political imaginaries that are structured by the clinic, she offers a critical sensibility in which psychoanalytic theories and practices are read through a political lens revealing the underlying situatedness of those theories in political and social ideologies.

In Chapter 1, Laubender places Anna Freud's child analysis at the forefront of politics and psychoanalysis. As a cofounder of child psychoanalysis, Freud's psychologizing orientation makes her a highly contentious figure in the psychoanalytic archive. Freud's “turn away from the excavation of the unconscious” (p. 39) toward ego psychology ran counter to the work of her father, Klein, Lacan, and others. Rigid in her analysis of children, Freud deemed them in need of “authoritative governance” (p. 39). Laubender suggests that Freud adopted a distinctly colonizing framework arguing that “the analyst's job is to ‘civilize’ the (presumably ‘savage‘) child” (p. 45).

Ben Kafka  

Donald Moss published his first sole-authored article in The Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 1986. Its title was “Pseudoquotes in Psychoanalytic Interventions,” and its topic was a peculiar verbal tic that some, if not all, analysts display at one time or another. The “pseudoquote,” Moss explained, occurs when “the analyst speaks for the patient, uses the pronoun ‘I,‘ as though he were, for the moment, the voice of the patient.” It might take the form of an echo, a paraphrase, or a best guess about what is going on in the patient's mind—spoken by the analyst as if it was the patient speaking. In such moments, Moss wrote:

The first person voice carries a second person predicate. For the moment, “I” is “you.” This odd configuration is transient; it passes quickly. It seems strategic, a result of some intention. It solves a problem. When the problem disappears, the “I” and “you” of the psychoanalytic dialogue return to their familiar positions.

Thirty-some years and thirty-plus articles later—not to mention several influential books—Moss returned to this primal intellectual scene with a book titled, simply, Iand You (Second Story Press, 2017). As a physical object, I and You was more of a chapbook, like something you might find in the poetry section of your local independent bookstore. Its content and layout also gave it the appearance of poetry. Only a brief note in the book's front matter explained otherwise:

Each day, for one year, at the end of every session, I wrote down one sentence my patient had said. Collated, placed, in the order they had been spoken, each sentence seemed to constitute a “line,” each day's sentences a “verse.”

In a sense, Moss was now enacting the dynamic that his article on pseudoquotes had once analyzed. The book presented a version of the psychoanalytic dialogue in which the “I” and the “you” were oddly configured, if only transiently, before returning us to our familiar positions.

Moss's latest book, Psychoanalysis in a Plague Year, extends this project, albeit under very different historical and epidemiological conditions. The image on the book's cover, by the British illustrator Nicholas Garland, shows us those familiar positions: chair, couch. But the furnishings are empty, the analyst and patient missing, and the psychoanalytic dialogue is on hold. Instead, in the background, we see death shouldering its scythe.

New Orleans, Louisiana, United States

John Rosegrant  

At the core of this book is a quietly radical extension of Winnicott's concept of transitional/potential space:

Winnicott gestures toward a radical implication, one he never fully acknowledges or develops: Imaginative elaboration … is a primary adaptive force throughout life. It does not distort reality—which is how “illusion” is often construed—but authentically animates what is apprehended. The imaginative arises from a vitalizing psychic sphere that exists prior to any distinction between fact and phantasy. Rather than gradually be superseded by a reality principle, it makes responsive use of the actual to engender an ongoing experiential sense of aliveness. What makes imaginative elaboration “primary” is that it is not an expression or derivative of, nor is it motivated by, anything else. It is what the psyche does to render experience emotionally meaningful. (17—italics added)

I italicized imaginative elaboration to highlight that this is the term Goldman uses, following Winnicott, to name the process of creating and maintaining an ongoing sense of aliveness. Goldman's understanding of imaginative elaboration is radical because he considers it to be its own form of thought, not derived from the pleasure or reality principles, and not the same as phantasy; imaginative elaboration precedes and influences the development of phantasy. Goldman deepens Winnicott's idea that one cannot meaningfully ask of transitional phenomena if they are real or imaginary; it is not that transitional phenomena are some intricate mixture of internal and external; rather, they are formed in a psychic process that is developmentally prior to and contributes to the recognition (or even the creation?) of internal and external worlds as separate.

Goldman discusses at least three roles that the imaginative plays in the psyche. The first is implicit in his title A Shimmering Landscape—imaginative elaboration makes the world come alive: “illusion is not a mixing, overlapping, or some combination of psychic contents and external reality, but an uncontested state of mind in its own right and a source of vitality throughout life” (p. 4). Second, imaginative elaboration enables us to conceive of different potential futures (p. 28). Third, in a complicated exploration of Winnicott's ideas about aggression and the use of an object (Chapter 6 and pp. 86–88), Goldman considers the imaginative to be “the primary drive” (88; here Goldman is not using “drive” in the usual psychoanalytic sense) that creates and maintains the not-me and me.

240 Greene Street, New York, NY 10003, United States

Katherine Jenness  

The centerpiece of Freud's British Family: Reclaiming Lost Lives in Manchester and London is a richly rendered depiction of Freud's two older half-brothers, Emanuel and Philip, and their lives and families in England. How did there come to be a British branch of the Freud family? The same economic precarity that led the traveling merchant Jacob Freud to depart Freiburg and ultimately settle in Vienna with young Sigmund in tow would lead the adult children of Jacob's first marriage to migrate to Manchester in search of better opportunities in the family trade. The now-separated families would remain in touch, visit occasionally, and appear in Willoughby's telling to have played large roles in each other's imaginations.

The great psychic importance of Freud's half-brothers is dramatized in The Interpretation of Dreams. Freud regarded Emanuel's children, his childhood playmates, as formative in shaping his sensitivity to Oedipal passions, and it is John and Pauline whom he emplots in his famous dream scene of yellow flowers and black bread. The Dreambook also reveals Freud's adornment of his younger half-brother Phillip in fantasies of Oedipal loss, as he was old enough to be his mother's husband (thus to be Freud's father) and was instrumental in separating Freud from his Catholic nanny by alerting the family to her petty theft. These figures receive their deserved biographical due in Freud's British Family. Willoughby also marshals fresh archival material to flesh out Freud's two visits to England (one when he was a solitary unknown, the later when he was a sought-after public figure) and his final migration to 20 Maresfield Gardens. Freud's imagining of his family was a crucible for his conception of mind.

Willoughby frames the material he offers through a bottom-up historical approach that seeks to elucidate the lives of lesser sung individuals whose experiences remain unremarked upon in the historical record. In this attention to lost lives, Willoughby invokes both the discourse of intersectionality, as well as the Joycean notion of heroics of the everyman who, like Leopold Bloom, plod through life the very best they can, given their circumstances. Overall, Willoughby's history contributes to the welcome departure from hagiography in Freud studies toward an emphasis on historical contextualization. It is also a part of a growing collection of careful scholarship that contextualizes psychoanalysis in Britain specifically, such as Forrester and Cameron's Freud in Cambridge (Forrester & Cameron, 2017), Michal Shapira's The War Inside: Psychoanalysis, Total War, and the Making of the Democratic Self in Postwar Britain (Shapira, 2015), and the scholarship of Carlolyn Laubender (e.g., Laubender, 2017, 2021).

Daniel Rosengart PsyD

Daniel Rosengart

303 5th Avenue #908, New York, NY 10016, United States
Email: daniel@rosengart.com

Daniel Rosengart  

The Mark Solms-helmed Revised Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud (RSE; Freud, 2024) is a major occasion for our field. While Solms retains almost all of Strachey's original translations, a significant new introduction and a remarkably thorough glossary of terms (complete with each term's histories in German and English and the ways different translators have taken on the words or phrases) provide invaluable scholarly context into the scientific and literary discourses of both Freud and his major translators. What emerges is unexpected, an RSE that, despite being almost word for word the same as its predecessor, reads very differently. Solms and his collaborators argue that Strachey's Standard Edition (SE), far from being the arbitrary and scientistic mistranslation it is often caricatured as, is a series of compromises that emerge from a profound wrestling with Freud's texts and with the traditions of German to English translations. This article seeks to summarize and draw out Solms's argument, while showing some of the ways that the RSE reads differently from the SE, and how the Freud that emerges in the RSE is subtly but radically different from the Freud in the SE.

There is no such thing as a new translation of Freud without controversy. From Brill, to the pre-Standard Edition era of many translators, to the Standard Edition, to the Adam Phillips-helmed Penguin Editions, each new translation is at least in part a rebuke of the previous one, and each new edition is a new Freud, drawn in the translator's image. And because psychoanalysts, whatever their institute and theory, must ultimately appeal to Freud's authority, each new translation must be greeted with anxious enthusiasm and curiosity, as well as an outpouring of critiques from everyone who appeals to a different Freud than the one found in the translation. Since there is no translation, perhaps in any language, with the reach and influence of the SE, no translation's shortcomings have been more extensively and variedly critiqued than Strachey's. English-speaking psychoanalysts can reliably be called upon to recite Strachey's greatest sins: Besetzung as cathexis; Nachträglichkeit as deferred action; Anlehnung as anaclitic; Trieb as drive; Ich, Über-Ich, and Es as Ego, Superego, and Id, etc.

Marilyn Charles PHD ABPP

Marilyn Charles

150 View Drive, Pittsfield, MA 01201, United States
Email: mcharlesphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1693-7207

Marilyn Charles  

metacognition, reflective function, personality disorders

People seek treatment at the Austen Riggs Center in the context of profound difficulties in adaptation that are marked diagnostically by mood disorders and personality disorders. Although mood and personality disorders have different diagnostic criteria, they cannot entirely be disentangled, each linked to dysfunctional parenting in childhood. Treatments that focus primarily on the depressive symptoms may miss the underlying difficulties with affect regulation linked to character pathology. I will link research and theory to clinical evidence to illustrate ways in which psychoanalytic therapy can be useful in enhancing mentalization in individuals with complex symptom pictures.

[[RunningHead]]

Epistemic Trust, Epistemic Vigilance, and Epistemic Injustice: Three Interrelated Constructs That Are Applicable to Psychodynamic Clinical Work

Elliot Jurist PHD PHD and Marissa Pizziferro MA

Marissa Pizziferro

160 Convent Avenue, New York, NY 10031, United States
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6103-1765
Email: ejurist5@gmail.com

Marissa Pizziferro  

epistemic trust, epistemic vigilance, epistemic injustice, mentalized affectivity, psychodynamic psychotherapy

The most important recent contribution to mentalization theory is the construct of epistemic trust. It serves as a bridge from the theory's roots in attachment to its current affirmation of the primacy of social communication. Epistemic trust refers to the capacity to trust in the authenticity and relevance of interpersonally transmitted knowledge. Epistemic trust evolves alongside a stance of epistemic vigilance, which, in many ways, works to buttress and reestablish epistemic trust. Epistemic vigilance is stimulated by ambiguity about trust and thus requires effort to determine whether epistemic trust should be granted or withheld. To date, the emphasis of epistemic trust and epistemic vigilance is on cognition, while the present article proposes that both trust and vigilance integrate cognition with emotion. Both epistemic trust and epistemic vigilance are linked to the construct of mentalized affectivity, the capacity to modulate emotions through reflection on autobiographical memory. Last, epistemic injustice refers to an exclusion, silencing, or undermining of one's capacity to be a knower. Commonly found in testimonials and within systems that minoritize certain identities and backgrounds, epistemic injustice is also prevalent in the context of psychotherapy even with the best intentioned and ethically informed therapists. Ultimately, this article shows how the three constructs can be utilized clinically. A case example is used to illustrate our understanding of each construct and to provide clinical implications for the psychodynamic psychotherapist. A mentalizing stance by the therapist is ultimately proposed as a counterbalance to epistemic injustice.

[[RunningHead]]

Dreams in Psychotherapy: An Empirically Supported Model of the Relations of Dreams to the Course of Psychotherapy

Christian Roesler PHD , Lisa Kissling MsSc , Till Sütterlin BSc and Alina Gees MsSc

Alina Gees

Karlsstr. 63, 79104 Freiburg, Germany
Email: christian.roesler@kh-freiburg.de
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3271-5521

Alina Gees  

dream interpretation, empirical dream research, clinical dream research, typical dream patterns, psychotherapy process

From the beginning, interpretation of dreams has been an integral part of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Nevertheless, it is still not clear how exactly dreams are related to psychopathology of the patient and the topics and course of therapy. This article aims at providing an empirically supported theoretical model of how dreams are specifically related to psychopathology, the topics that are focused on in psychotherapy, and the general course and results of psychotherapy. On the background of the findings of empirical and clinical dream research, the methodology of Structural Dream Analysis is presented which has reached the point of formulating a theoretical model which explains the relations between dreams and improvement gained in the course of psychotherapy. The core concept that was found is a hierarchical typology of dream patterns which are characterized by different levels of agency of the dream ego representing the extent of ego strength of the person. The central hypothesis contained in the theoretical model of a correlation between a rise in dream ego agency parallel to improvement in psychotherapy is statistically tested and found to be confirmed. The methodology of Structural Dream Analysis has the potential to inform future research but can also be used in assessment and clinical practice in the field of psychotherapeutic dream work.

Treatment-Resistant Depression and Personality Disorders: An Update on Conceptualization, Assessment, and Evidence-Based Psychodynamic Approaches

Vitaliy Voytenko PsyD and Steven Huprich PHD

Steven Huprich

7 Blanchard Circle, Suite 201, Wheaton, IL 60189, United States

Wheaton, Illinois, United States

Steven Huprich  

treatment-resistant depression, personality disorder, evidence-based treatment, transference-focused psychotherapy, mentalization-based treatment

When an individual diagnosed with major depressive disorder does not respond sufficiently even after multiple adequate trials of otherwise effective treatments, the label treatment-resistant depression (TRD) is often used to describe the patient's condition. Personality disorders are among the most prevalent comorbid psychiatric conditions in major depressive disorder and, when undiagnosed or untreated, can contribute to the development of treatment resistance. This article provides an update on conceptualization and assessment of comorbid TRD and personality disorders and introduces three available evidence-based psychodynamic approaches to treatment: good psychiatric management, transference-focused psychotherapy, and mentalization-based treatment. This lead article for the special section on TRD and personality disorders is followed by companion articles on transference-focused psychotherapy, mentalization-based treatment, and mentalization more broadly.

[[RunningHead]]

Transference-Focused Psychotherapy for Treatment-Resistant Depression and Comorbid Personality Disorders

Kenneth Levy PHD and Daniel Spina MA MS

Daniel Spina

362 Bruce V. Moore Building, University Park, PA 16802, United States
Email: klevy@psu.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4670-4631

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4015-7486

Daniel Spina  

major depressive disorder, comorbidity, personality disorder, transference-focused psychotherapy

Research indicates that treatment-resistant depression is common in clinical practice. Although there are several reasons why depression can be difficult to ameliorate, one common reason stems from comorbid personality disorders. Research has shown that not only are personality disorders commonly comorbid among those with depression, but their presence negatively affects the course and outcome in the treatment of depression. This implies that careful assessment of comorbid personality pathology is of critical importance in the case conceptualization of treatment-resistant depression and may be essential for planning treatments that most benefit patients. In the following article, we illustrate the value of transference-focused psychotherapy (TFP), an evidence-based approach for treatment of personality disorders, in addressing treatment-resistant depression when there is a comorbid personality disorder. To do this, we discuss the evidence regarding the comorbidity of treatment-resistant depression and personality disorders and the implications for treatment approaches. We make an argument for treatments such as TFP. We then explicate the TFP model of treatment, including TFP's model of personality pathology in the development of treatment-resistant depression. In doing so, we also describe the evidence base behind TFP and its mechanisms of change. Clinical vignettes are also used to give clear examples of how TFP assesses and treats treatment-resistant depression in the context of personality pathology.

Exploring Object Relations and Self-Structure in Men With Substance Use Disorders: A Qualitative Study

Tina Amiri MSc , Frank Summers PHD , Samaneh Ghafoori PHD and Mahmood Dehghani PHD

Mahmood Dehghani

325 North Wells Street, Chicago, IL 60654, United States
Email: tinaamiri888@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-7581-4314

Mashhad, Iran

Mahmood Dehghani  

addiction, object relations, psychoanalytic approach, qualitative study, content analysis

Psychoanalytic perspectives consider that object relations and deficits of self-structure play a significant role in the etiology of substance use disorders. However, despite the rich psychoanalytic literature addressing this issue, there is only a limited amount of research supportive of these approaches. This study aimed to illustrate a comprehensive picture of object-relational patterns and deficits affecting the self-structure of substance abusers. The research was conducted based on a qualitative method and the content analysis method of Graneheim and Lundman's approach, and 23 patients with substance use disorder participated in it. Finally, four themes were identified as follows: relational patterns, quality of representations, defense mechanisms, and filling an inner void.

Complex Depression: The Role of Personality Dynamics and Social Ecology, by Golan Shahar. Washington, DC: American Psychological Association, 2023. 249 pp., $89.99 (ISBN 9781433836077).

John Auerbach PHD

John Auerbach

3300 South University Drive, Fort Lauderdale, FL 33328-2004, United States
Email: jauerba1@nova.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8390-9786

John Auerbach  

How often have you, as a psychoanalytic clinician, had a conversation with a medication provider who proceeds to tell you that your severely depressed patient is suffering from chemical imbalance—a statement that is necessarily true insofar as all mental states are brain states and all brain states involve chemical processes but that is also trivial? Or, how often have you been told by a cognitive-behavioral colleague that your patient's chronic depression derives from dysfunctional or, worse yet, irrational beliefs? These questions are purely rhetorical, of course, a lead-in to the matter of Golan Shahar's new book, Complex Depression. In this relatively brief but insightful volume, a work of uncommon honesty because it begins its author's revelation that he lost to suicide the man who was his stepfather but who for all intents and purposes was his father, Shahar begins by explaining that clinical depression, not the mood state but the syndrome found in diagnostic manuals, is more likely to be complex, by which he means having recurrences, comorbidities, various interpersonal and social dysfunctions, and a high risk for suicide, than simple, meaning a single episode without comorbidity, significant interpersonal dysfunction, or significant suicidal risk. Noncomplex symptoms and course appear to account for less than 10% of depressions. Acknowledging briefly the necessary contribution of neurobiology to any depressive process and therefore affirming a biopsychosocial model, Shahar devotes the remainder of this work to an explication of the many factors that render depression far more likely to be complex than to be simple. Relying on Sidney Blatt's two-configurations model, Shahar argues that the major personality factor in complex depression is self-criticism, and he places this factor at the center of what he terms a reformulated depressive position (RDP), an organization of self-states that creates a vicious circle of self-criticism, depressed mood, interpersonal dysfunction, and more self-criticism. He situates the individual patient in Bronfenbrenner's ecological model and proposes that developments in any of five ecological levels, from microsystems that effect the patient directly through, at the broadest levels, the macrosystem (society as a whole) and the chronosystem (the entire set of systems as they change in history or over time), can either exacerbate or attenuate an individual's depressive difficulties. Finally, he proposes a psychodynamic psychotherapeutic approach that involves elements of existentialism and cognitive therapy and that is active and directive in challenging the rigidity of an entrenched RDP.

Exigent Psychoanalysis: The Interventions of Jean Laplanche, by Gila Ashtor. New York, NY: Routledge, 2021. 200 pp., $42.95 (ISBN 9781032039770).

Elaine Zickler PHD LCSW

Elaine Zickler

26 East Oak Avenue, Moorestown, NJ 08057, United States
Email: mezickler@gmail.com

Elaine Zickler  

Gila Ashtor, PhD, LP, is a psychoanalyst, writer, and critic on the faculty at Columbia University. In writing about the work of Jean Laplanche, she joins a small group of psychoanalysts in the avant-garde of theory here in North America where Laplanche's work has only reached an English-speaking audience in the past 2 decades or so thanks, mainly, to the translations of John Fletcher and Jonathan House and his associates at Unconscious in Translation Press.

Dr. Ashtor is a careful and thorough scholar of psychoanalysis whose project in this book is to stage “an encounter between Laplanche and the field of Contemporary Anglo-American psychoanalysis as it exists today” (p. 34). The book is structured around four major concepts and areas of conflict in the field: the mytho-symbolic and metapsychology, sexuality and the unconscious, seduction and psychic structure, and translation and motivation. She begins by posing two questions to psychoanalytic theory since Freud: Why has theory not been more forthcoming about its divergences from Freud, as it seems clear that thinking and practice have changed radically without, nonetheless, a concomitant radical change in the language of theory itself? And, how can we account for the allegiance to Freud himself as a figure, a synecdoche for psychoanalytic theory and practice?

Ashtor observes that Freud has commanded an allegiance to his person that subsequent generations of psychoanalysts have been loath to openly challenge for all the usual Oedipal reasons but more because of the fact that the field itself has ingrained and self-perpetuating “habits of looking externally, rather than internally, for sources of the field's flaws.” As examples of these externally imposed “flaws,” Ashtor cites both positivist science and postmodern philosophy (p. 7). While her introduction of the interventions of Jean Laplanche goes a long way toward correcting these ingrained habits, at least theoretically, her own turn to affect theory and to neuroscience for validation would seek external rather than internal validation for the field, something of an inversion of the above critique. This begs the question of whether psychoanalysis can ever define itself and the object of its inquiry without inevitably making such turns, even metaphorically, and whether psychoanalysis does not yield its unique object of inquiry in so doing. One thinks of the ways in which Freud's use of biology inevitably upended his own radical notion of the drive as decidedly not instinctual or of the mythic turn he took in naming the life and death drives, Eros and Thanatos. Ashtor appeals to what she names the “Sinai complex,” locating the problem squarely in Freud's own identification with Moses the lawgiver and his followers' fears of betraying him and being abandoned in the wilderness of a psychoanalysis without Freud. The question about allegiance to Freud, the man, is itself a diversion and a recentering, one that Laplanche avoided in his work, focusing instead on the problem of theory itself. Ashtor is very strong in pointing this out as one of Laplanche's virtues, his ability to separate “Freud” as man and as a totemic object from his own theory, and to use this separation to show how Freud himself went “astray” from his own discoveries repeatedly over the course of his career. This observation of Laplanche's, while crucial to his deconstruction of Freud's work, is brilliant because it is generalizable to the motions of the psyche itself, as well as to the oscillations of psychoanalytic theory and to the political and cultural spheres.

Carla Sharp  

personality, depression, psychodynamic, comorbid

In recent decades, the evidence base for psychodynamic psychotherapy has expanded considerably, allowing for the application of treatments like transference-focused and mentalization-based therapies to a host of psychiatric concerns across a variety of contexts (Bateman & Fonagy, 2004, 2008, 2015, 2016; Constantinides & Dauphin, 2023; Griffiths et al., 2019; Yeomans et al., 2015). As that evidence base expands, we find that these therapies are particularly applicable to individuals with comorbid personality disorders and treatment-resistant depression. We aim in this special section to review the ways this is supported by research, with particular focus on mentalization-based treatment. This section will begin by stating the relevance of mentalizing to good mental health and, further, how comorbid personality pathology and treatment-resistant depression present distinct challenges to mentalizing. Then we elaborate on imbalances in mentalizing, how these difficulties underpin personality pathology, amplify depression, and manifest differently across personality types. This leads to a review of mentalization-based approaches, namely the more structured treatment model and the informal application of a mentalizing framework. To illustrate the effect of a treatment using the mentalizing framework, a case example is offered using a novel method of mentalizing assessment (incomplete sentence task). The use of three regularly administered sentence completion tasks at the beginning, middle, and end of treatment observed linguistic and qualitative changes in the patient's mentalizing. We close with conclusions about this novel method and the potential for mentalization-based approaches for those with comorbid personality pathology and treatment-resistant depression, with ideas for future directions.

Svenja Taubner  

mentalization, reflective functioning, alliance, longitudinal analysis, process study

The therapeutic alliance is a well-established predictor of psychotherapy outcome, and the ability to mentalize has been discussed as a change mechanism in psychotherapy. Low mentalization may lead to impairments in the alliance. In the present study, we assessed mentalization effects on the therapeutic alliance over the course of treatment. Thirty-seven patients diagnosed with depression or anxiety disorders received 25 ± 3 sessions of integrative cognitive behavioral therapy (outpatient setting). Patient's mentalization was coded by external raters based on transcripts of the first, eighth, 16th, and 24th therapy sessions, and the alliance quality was assessed from both patient and therapist perspectives after each session. The effects of mentalization on the alliance were investigated both within and between patients using hierarchical linear modeling. A higher patient ability to mentalize was related to a better therapist-reported but not patient-reported alliance during treatment. Mentalization did not show significant within-patient effects on the therapeutic alliance. To conclude, therapists may perceive a stronger therapeutic alliance with patients who exhibit a greater capacity for mentalization. It might be important for therapists to pay attention to a possible positive bias when patients present with a high ability to mentalize.

Leonie Kampe  

countertransference, therapeutic relationship, defense mechanisms, therapeutic emotional reaction, personality traits

Countertransference (CT) as a specific aspect of the therapeutic relationship plays a crucial role in psychotherapy. It serves as a source of clinical information for initial diagnostics and is relevant over the course of the therapeutic process. Theory and research have found that some personality features in patients are significantly related to specific CT reactions in the therapists. In this interplay, the role of patients’ defense mechanisms (DM) has not been sufficiently investigated. This study aimed to explore the association between patients’ DMs and therapists’ CTs in a psychiatric inpatient sample. DMs were assessed by the Defense Style Questionnaire in 71 patients. Twelve therapists used the Therapist Response Questionnaire to rate their CT toward their patients throughout the treatment. Considering the nested data structure with therapists on Level 2 and patients on Level 1, Pearson correlations between patients’ DMs and therapists’ CTs were calculated. After Benjamini–Hochberg correction, six correlations remained significant: Patients’ DM humor was positively correlated with therapists’ positive/satisfying and sexualized CT and negatively with disengaged CT. DM acting out was positively correlated with helpless/inadequate and hostile/mistreated CT. DM splitting showed the strongest positive correlation with overwhelmed/disorganized CT and was also positively associated with special/overinvolved and hostile/mistreated CT. DM isolation was positively associated with special/overinvolved CT, and somatization was negatively associated with sexualized CT. Under careful consideration of the methodological limitations of our study, the relevance of our findings for the therapeutic relationship and process is discussed, and further research questions are pointed out.

Magdalena Konop MD , Jerzy Sobański MD PHD , Katarzyna Klasa MA PHD , Krzysztof Rutkowski MD , Michał Mielimąka MD PHD and and Edyta Dembińska MD PHD

and Edyta Dembińska

and Edyta Dembińska  

electrodermal activity, heart rate, psychophysiology, psychodynamic psychotherapy

This study is an exploratory analysis of trends in the physiological responses of both the psychotherapist and the patient by examining heart rate and electrodermal activity during psychodynamic psychotherapy sessions. The study involved 24 patients diagnosed with neurotic disorders (F40-F48) and/or personality disorders (F60-F61) according to the International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision criteria. The mean age of participants was 32.4 years (SD = 7.2). The sample consisted of 62.5% women (n = 15) and 37.5% men (n = 9). Data were collected from 96 psychodynamic psychotherapy sessions led by two psychotherapists. The statistical analysis involved constructing regression models for four physiological variables: heart rate, skin conductance level, skin conductance response frequency, and skin conductance response amplitude for patients and psychotherapists. Subsequently, scatter plots depicting these variables for patients and psychotherapists were juxtaposed for comparison. Additionally, correlation analysis was performed. The trends in physiological responses in the regression analysis differed significantly: Heart rate remained stable for therapists but decreased for patients, skin conductance level increased for both patients and therapists but rose more sharply for therapists, skin conductance response frequency decreased for patients but remained stable for therapists, and skin conductance response amplitude remained constant for patients but increased for therapists. Correlation analysis revealed that, over a psychotherapy session, all physiological parameters of the psychotherapists increased, while all those of the patients decreased. The findings indicate significantly divergent trends in physiological activity between psychotherapists and patients during psychotherapy sessions, suggesting an antiphase physiological synchrony trend.

Measuring Personality Change in Psychoanalytic Treatments: Opportunities for the Use of the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual

Katie Aafjes-van Doorn PHD , Daniel Spina MSc , Sarah Horne MSc , Bernard Gorman PHD , Karl Stukenberg PHD , Francesco Gazzillo PHD , Federica Genova PHD and Sherwood Waldron MD

Sherwood Waldron

567 West Yangsi Road, Pudong New Area, Shanghai 200126, China
E-Mail: kav9239@nyu.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2584-5897

New York, New York, United States

Sherwood Waldron  

Psychoanalytic treatment endeavors to improve patients’ psychological health and functioning and ameliorate personality pathology. However, these treatment outcomes are notoriously hard to capture via self-report measures and not frequently measured in naturalistic psychoanalytic practice. In this brief report, we examined the validity of the Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual’s Psychodiagnostic Chart–2 (PDC-2; R. M. Gordon & Bornstein, 2015) in assessing patients’ psychological health and personality pathology achieved in 27 long-term psychoanalytic treatments in naturalistic practice. We used previously collected session recordings (eight early- and eight late-phase sessions per treatment) that had previously been rated by observers on the Shedler–Westen Assessment Procedure-200 (Shedler & Westen, 1998). A group of graduate student observers rated the PDC-2. Results indicated that multiple indices of the PDC-2 showed statistically significant change from early to late in treatment. The PDC-2 was also related to ratings on the Shedler–Westen Assessment Procedure-200 at both the early and late phases of treatment, supporting the convergent validity of the measure.

Wei Zhang  

As Govrin observes, philosophy has deeply influenced psychoanalysis over time, shaping it to varying extents and in different ways: “When the scientific ethos was dominant, psychoanalysis was ‘scientific.’ When modern hermeneutics became popular, it became ‘hermeneutic.’ When the influence of postmodernism was evident, psychoanalysis became ‘postmodern’” (p. 192). This underscores the necessity of engaging with philosophy when discussing psychoanalysis. Philosophy, as a pursuit of the “love of wisdom,” is fundamentally concerned with examining unexamined premises. In his book, Govrin integrates significant contributions from the philosophy of science and systematically examines psychoanalysis through the lens of Freud's theories, classical psychoanalysis, self psychology, the intersubjective approach, the relational approach, and neuropsychoanalysis, presenting these in chronological order and offering profound analyses. Notably, Govrin points out that “most analysts have no background or training in philosophy” (p. 164), leaving them unaware of how these unexamined premises influence their theoretical frameworks and clinical practices. In this sense, the book offers analysts a valuable opportunity to develop new perspectives and refresh their understanding of their discipline's foundational concepts. When I came across the book's contents, I was immediately captivated by its approach and scope.

In the introduction, the author employs a metaphor to illustrate the relationship between psychoanalysis and philosophy,

One of the children (i.e., psychoanalysis) has chosen to turn away from the family (i.e., philosophy) in order to find itself a new family (in the shape of medical and biological science). [However,] perceived as a foreign body and a stepchild, the child does not truly fit in with its chosen family. (p. 7)

In my view, this metaphor aptly captures the complexity of their relationship. Philosophy, often regarded as the mother of all disciplines, has given rise to numerous fields—such as physics, chemistry, and psychology—that have gradually established their independence. Psychoanalysis, however, occupies a unique position, existing at the intersection of psychology and philosophy. This dual affiliation offers valuable insights into the historical development and contemporary status of psychoanalysis.

This review of Your Mum and Dad, a profoundly moving film, examines the intergenerational transmission of trauma through two stories and their respective healing using psychoanalysis and art, respectively. The first story brings us into the clinic as we observe an interracial analysis of a Jewish man whose mother lost her family in the Holocaust. The second is the filmmaker's story whose parents maintained a 50-year silence over the death of her sister who drowned at the age of 4. The film artistically demonstrates how the search for truth is both painful and liberating.

This film is framed by the poem “This Be The Verse,” by the late British poet, Philip Larkin:

They fuck you up, your mum and dad.

They may not mean to, but they do.

They fill you with the faults they had

And add some extra, just for you.

But they were fucked up in their turn

By fools in old-style hats and coats,

Who half the time were soppy-stern

And half at one another's throats.

Man hands on misery to man.

Sam Semper  

Saketopoulou and Pellegrini's (2023) Gender Without Identity has emerged at a time of intense cultural anxiety in which gender has once again become a battleground in the public sphere and in psychoanalytic debates. Trans lives, in particular, have become the object of moral panic mobilizing regulatory anxiety in increasingly violent ways. At a time when institutional psychoanalysis is under pressure to recognize the ways in which social and psychic reality are imbricated and held accountable for a profoundly difficult history of encounters with queer and trans people, gender has perhaps not surprisingly become a flashpoint. It is not an exaggeration to say that Gender Without Identity has become a central text in these discussions. Thanks to the urgency of the writing and the authors’ labor to share their ideas with a wider audience, the work has migrated into other spheres and taken on a life of its own online. Rarely does a psychoanalytic text have such an effect within and outside of the psychoanalytic community.

A Queer Volume

Gender Without Identity is built around an expanded version of the authors’ award-winning article, “A Feminine Boy: Normative Investments and Reparative Fantasy at the Intersection of Gender, Race, and Religion,” which was awarded the inaugural Sexual Diversity Committee's Tiresias Prize at the 2021 Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association. Originally intended to be published in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis, it was released as a book after the removal of sections of the article deemed “too political” by the editors became a condition of its publication in that journal (p. xi). As the authors describe, the editorial requirement to remove an acknowledgment that spoke to the violence experienced by queer and trans analysts and analysands and welcomed trans and queer folks as peers and colleagues led to an impasse and to a version of the article being published in a separate volume. The disputed passages are included as an extended footnote with the “offending” text highlighted in yellow, just as they were sent to the authors (p. ix). These streaks of color disrupt any easy reading experience.

Golan Shahar  

My own original training and background was in the psychodynamic tradition, and psychodynamic thought, especially in its newer relational forms, continues to inform and enrich my work to this day. But my psychodynamic sensibility owes as much to Robin Hood as to Freud. I steal reaches from other orientations, and I do so openly and brazenly. And, like Robin Hood (at least the Robin Hood of myth), my aim is to give away the fruits of my thieving to those in need—in this case, my patients. (P. L. Wachtel, 2023, p. 4)

Psychotherapy is a single word, but it houses many meanings. (P. L. Wachtel, 2023, p. 279)

When Paul Wachtel's new book was published, I knew I would not only be reading it very intently but that I would also be interested in reviewing it, and in a. The latter is far from being straightforward because Wachtel's work appeals to numerous audiences beyond psychoanalytic psychologists: to humanistic psychologists, cognitive behavioral therapists, family therapists, and—most obviously— integrationists. Why I wanted to review it and why for a psychoanalytic venue was quite clear to me: Wachtel's writings constitute a formidable cornerstone of my own intellectual–professional family. His doctoral dissertation advisor—the late Sidney J. Blatt—is my own mythological mentor (Shahar & Mayes, 2017). Wachtel's writings were the ones stirring me toward psychotherapy integration (Shahar, 2004), and—perhaps most relevant here—they add grist to the neverending mill of my own love–rage relationship with psychoanalysis. Hence, in initiating this review, I knew that the task would be absorbing, exhilarating, arduous, and most of all meaningful. I hope that this review conveys all of these in a way that pays the book the respect it deserves.

The book constitutes Wachtel's latest iteration in a fascinating journey toward a comprehensive integration of psychoanalytic and nonpsychoanalytic psychotherapeutic schools of thought. Wachtel's previous work entailed an integration of interpersonal and relational psychoanalysis (Aron, 1996; Horney, 1937; Mitchell, 1988; Sullivan, 1953), attachment theory (Bowlby, 1988; Fonagy, 2001; Slade, 2014), political–economic thought (Fromm, 1955; P. L. Wachtel, 1983, 2003, 2014), and behavior therapy (Wilson, 1997; Wolpe, 1958). In this book, he adds two fresh ingredients to this integrative mix: phenomenological psychotherapy (i.e., Leslie Greenberg's emotional-focused therapy; Greenberg, 2017) and third-wave cognitive behavioral therapy (in particular, acceptance and commitment therapy [ACT], Hayes, 2004; dialectical behavior therapy, Heard & Linehan, 1994). Central to this novel, multitier integrative iteration is a new/old term invoked by Wachtel: the disavowed self. This term pertains to a wealth of self-related experiences, emanating in early childhood and amalgamated throughout the life cycle—which are censored by the subject (agent) because they activate internal distress, primarily shame. The disavowed self seeps through this censorship in the form of symptoms and problem behaviors that create havoc in patients’ lives but also—quite fortunately—bring them to therapy. It is important to clarify, already in this early phase of this review, that the disavowed self is neither “the repressed self” nor “the dissociated self.” Disavowal is a very subtle self-regulatory maneuver (a defense mechanism) that appears in Freud's writing (Breuer & Freud, 1957), albeit largely in passing as an alternative to unconscious denial. As previously noted (Shahar & Schiller, 2016), disavowal pertains to “the person deliberately dimming his/her consciousness with respect to known facts, or what Zespf (2013) labels ‘a type of lying that is not consciously intended (p. 36)’” (Shahar & Schiller, 2016, p. 31). The distinction is important because, according to P. L. Wachtel (2023), the disavowed self—and the following words are my own—stands between awareness and oblivion, perhaps between life and death. It is neither here nor there. Almost within reach but absent. And this subtle problem needs a subtle solution amounting to the integration of experience-near exploratory psychoanalytic work, augmented by expressive phenomenological techniques that free patients to express their own emotionemotions and by third-wave cognitive behavioral therapy techniques that enable the patient to accept what has just been expressed. Thus, argues Wachtel, the disavowed self does not come to life by brutal extraction. Rather, it (hesitantly) steps into a room once it begins to believe there is one available. The therapeutic work is likened to a search and rescue mission, which, while heroic, is nevertheless gentle.

Sex on the Spectrum: Sexuality's Potential to Free Up Autistic Subjectivity

Diana Moga MD PHD

Diana Moga

139 West 82nd Street, Suite 1CD, New York, NY 10024, United States
E-Mail: Diana@Dianamogamd.com

Diana Moga  

autism, psychoanalysis, sexuality

Autistic adults are scantly written about psychoanalytically, and when they are, their difference is acknowledged as deficit, a deficit which diminishes their subjecthood and potential and therefore also sexuality. This article will investigate the way that sexuality in its creative and transgressive potential can free up autistic pleasure, agency, and connection in transformative ways that resist the pathologizing meanings imposed on autistic bodyminds by allistic projections. Autistics have the right to their libidinal strivings and the freedom to constitute their subjectivity through their own search for meaning. Our work is to facilitate the process of authentic becoming or autopoiesis that may have been stalled by social trauma and stigmatization and allistic society's lack of mentalization of autistic experience. I will discuss barriers in both analyst and analysand to this process and illustrate the challenges I had to overcome in order to empathically enter my autistic patient's lived experience and see his sexual strivings through a new and now shared perspective, which in turn allowed him to engage in meaningful psychic work.

Mapping Childhood Trauma and Personality Organization: A Network Approach

Jürgen Fuchshuber PHD , Human-Friedrich Unterrainer PsyD PHD , Nestor Kapusta MD PHD and Victor Blüml MD PHD

Victor Blüml

Grüner Kreis Society, Vienna, Austria

Schenkenstraße 8-10/5th floor, 1010 Vienna, Austria
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4815-0214
Email: human.unterrainer@univie.ac.at

Victor Blüml  

personality organization, childhood trauma, primitive defense mechanisms, emotional abuse, network analysis

Significant research has been dedicated to the relation between childhood adversities and the development of personality structure. The present research aims to contribute to this exploration using a data-driven network approach. Specifically, the study psychometrically investigates the relationships between personality organization (PO) and childhood trauma (CT). A total sample of 1,110 participants from the general population (68% female) was analyzed in terms of personality organization (IPO-16) and childhood trauma (CTQ) within a pairwise Markov random fields model. The network was estimated using the EBICglasso algorithm. Regularized partial correlation edge weights, node centrality indices (estimated influence, strength, bridge strength), and predictability were examined. Significant correlations were observed between PO and CT across all subdomains (p < .001). The analysis of the regularized partial correlation network revealed a significant edge between primitive defense and emotional abuse, representing a bridge between PO and CT. This finding was supported by the high centrality indices of both emotional abuse and primitive defense. The findings suggest that the relationship between CT and PO is primarily mediated by the link between primitive defense mechanisms and experiences of emotional abuse in childhood. The implications of these psychometric results are discussed within the context of psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice.

Examining an Assumption in Object-Relational Personality Diagnosis: Does Style Imply Severity?

Benjamin Berry PHD and Felicia Wolf PsyD

Felicia Wolf

One Baylor Plaza, BCM350, Houston, TX 77030, United States
Email: bberry1@menninger.edu

Felicia Wolf  

object relations, personality pathology, personality organization, personality style, clinical severity

Influenced in part by object relations theory, contemporary models of personality pathology are converging in their emphasis on the central role of the self and interpersonal functioning. However, they have largely not adopted object relations theory's assumption that personality disorder style and personality disorder severity are linked. The present study was designed to investigate the relationship between personality disorder style and severity in an inpatient adolescent sample. Categorical personality disorder diagnoses were converted into (a) personality style groups, permitting group comparisons, and (b) a dimensional personality style score, permitting correlation analyses with measures of personality organization and clinical severity. Group differences and correlation analyses both demonstrated that personality disorder style is associated with clinical severity in our sample. Mediation analyses further demonstrated that, for three of four clinical severity constructs, the relationships between personality style and clinical severity were mediated by differences in personality organization impairment. Results were broadly supportive of hypotheses, providing support for the object-relational model of personality diagnosis.

Rosetta Castellano  

attachment, disorganization, fear, couple's bond, recognition

The purpose of this article is to enrich and elucidate the connections between attachment research and psychoanalytic practice, linking Bowlby's psychoanalytic origins with the influential body of research produced in this field. The findings on attachment disorganization will be examined from two standpoints. The first regards what is “behind” it, namely the origins of disorganized attachment, as illustrated by Beatrice Beebe's research on the precursors of attachment patterns identified from microanalytic nonverbal communication between 4-month-old infants and their mothers. The second pertains to what is “beyond” it, namely the trajectories of attachment disorganization after childhood and specifically in the context of the couple bond, where disorganized attachment patterns may be observed. Drawing on Bowlby's proposal that conflict arises when two or more incompatible systems are at play, this article will propose that the compelling need of being seen and known frequently coexists with fear, activating the attachment system in contradictory ways. The article will then explore the clinical value of considering attachment and recognition in their entanglements. A clinical vignette will illustrate the ways in which these systems may act simultaneously, determining conflict, competition, dissociation, and even disorganized/disorganizing states.

Ahmet Sayım MA , Yasemin İlkmen PsyD and Sibel Halfon PHD

Sibel Halfon  

transference intervention, therapeutic alliance, adolescence

Transference interpretations have the potential to address unconscious interpersonal conflicts that find reflection in the therapy relationship and improve the patient's psychosocial functioning; however, they should be practiced in the context of a strong therapeutic alliance so as to prevent adverse effects. Despite their conceptual relations, the impact of the therapeutic alliance on transference work in the treatment of adolescents has not been previously examined extensively. This study was the first to examine whether a strong therapeutic alliance predicted the use of transference interventions in a single case of adolescent psychodynamic psychotherapy. The case of the study was a 16-year-old Turkish adolescent male, with anxiety and attention problems. A total of 57 sessions that cover his entire treatment were transcribed and coded for both therapeutic alliance via the Therapy Process Observational Coding System–Alliance Scale and transference interventions by the Transference Work Scale. Time Series Granger Causality Tests indicated that transference interventions that address the therapist directly in the interpretation were used only when therapeutic alliance was stronger. Strong therapeutic alliance may provide the necessary context for the therapist to use transference interpretations that contain elements of the here-and-now relationship.

[[RunningHead]]

Empathy and Lacanian Psychoanalysis? A Qualitative Study

Stephanie Swales PHD , Christopher Bell PHD and John Roberts PHD

John Roberts

Glasnevin, Dublin 9, D09 W6Y4, Ireland
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6299-4619
Email: stephanie.swales@dcu.ie

John Roberts  

empathy, Lacan, research, psychoanalysis, qualitative

In contrast to other major schools of psychoanalysis, Lacanian psychoanalysis views the concept of empathy with circumspection because, as a way of knowing something about the analysand's experience, it is seen to operate in the Imaginary order; that is, empathy from this perspective understands the other's experience on the basis of one's own experience, thus colonizing the other's difference and obscuring the domains of the unconscious and the Real, or that which escapes and resists being symbolized. The present research seeks to critically question and complicate this view of empathy in Lacanian psychoanalysis. Using transcriptions of our qualitative interviews with 12 practicing Lacanian analysts about their views on empathy and what place if any it has in their analytic practice, we conducted a reflexive thematic analysis to discern how empathy may play a number of unacknowledged roles in Lacanian psychoanalysis and/or be used in novel ways that might contribute to the theorizations of empathy within Lacanian as well as other traditions of psychoanalysis. To interpret the analysts' viewpoints, we used Lacanian discourse theory because it provides an account of how Lacanian analysts listen and intervene in analytic practice. We applied a reflexive thematic analysis to identify four themes: empathy in the Imaginary can be harmful, empathy in the Imaginary can be helpful, empathy exercised from the analyst's position in the Symbolic can honor the difference of the analysand, and such an empathy can be attuned toward the Real or the impossible but without claiming to understand.

[[RunningHead]]

Filling the Emptiness: An Empirically Supported and Psychodynamic-Oriented Single Case Study on Structural Change in Binge-Eating Disorder Treatment

Marta Mirabella PHD , Bianca Di Giannantonio PHD , Nicola Carone PHD , Anna Franco MS , Michele Rugo MD , Claudia Mazzeschi PHD , Tom Wooldridge PHD , Vittorio Lingiardi MD and Laura Muzi PHD

Laura Muzi

Piazza G. Ermini 1, 06123 Perugia, Italy
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6394-6018
Email: laura.muzi@unipg.it

Laura Muzi  

binge-eating disorder, personality change, defensive functioning, body image, single case

Binge-eating disorder (BED) is characterized by recurrent episodes of binge eating involving the consumption of large amounts of food accompanied by a marked loss of control. Individuals with BED tend to exhibit affect dysregulation, identity diffusion, use of immature defense mechanisms, and difficulties distinguishing between mental and somatic states. The present study examined the clinical case of a 25-year-old patient with a Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders-5-TR diagnosis of BED, who was receiving treatment at a specialized residential treatment center. Upon intake, the patient presented with an insecure-preoccupied attachment style, avoidant personality disorder with dependent and schizoid traits, low defensive functioning, and marked symptom impairment. A comprehensive psychodynamic-oriented treatment, incorporating a multimethod and multi-informant approach, resulted in meaningful structural and symptomatic improvements, particularly in personality organization, defensive patterns, and symptom severity. The findings underscore the clinical importance of the structural reorganization of core areas of functioning in patients with BED, necessitating an individual approach to treatment.

Test Selection in Psychoanalytically Informed Assessment

Alina Kopetzki MS , Mark Blais PsyD , Hilde de Saeger PHD , Stephen Finn PHD , Katie Lewis PHD and Christopher Hopwood PHD

Christopher Hopwood

Austin, Texas, United States

Stockbridge, Massachusetts, United States

Binzmuehlestrasse 14, 8050 Zurich, Switzerland
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-6645-8645
Email: chopwoodmsu@gmail.com

Christopher Hopwood  

psychological assessment, therapeutic assessment, test selection, psychodynamic, psychoanalytic

Psychological assessment involves the use of validated measurement tools to answer specific questions. However, there are a wide range of available tools, a variety of reasons for choosing them in any given clinical context, and very little evidence to guide instrument selection. Experienced clinicians may have intuitions about which tests to use to answer specific questions, and psychoanalytic theory provides a structure within which to consider what kinds of tests to use in different clinical situations. In this article, we review common considerations for selecting tests in clinical assessment from a psychoanalytic perspective. We then use a case to illustrate how four expert assessors who work in different contexts and have somewhat different backgrounds demonstrate their approach to test selection in answering specific collaborative assessment questions. Similarities and differences between these experts are discussed, with an eye toward developing a more systematic model for test selection that could be helpful to psychodynamic assessors and provide a foundation for applied research.

[[RunningHead]]

Erotics of the Act: Psychoanalysis and Feminist Activism in Latin America

Carolina Arrau PHD

Carolina Arrau

Colectivo Trenza, Monseñor Felix Cabrera 14, of 51, Providencia, Santiago 7500021, Chile
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-5149-6646
Email: caritobesoain@gmail.com

Carolina Arrau  

psychoanalysis, action, feminist activism, erotics, gender

This article offers a psychoanalytic exploration of acts as erotic and transformative forces, foregrounding the impact of intersectional feminist activism on psychoanalysis in Latin America over the past decade. While psychoanalysis has often approached the “realm of action” with caution—frequently framing it as forms of acting out or failures of symbolization—this article proposes an alternative to understand acts as openings charged with erotic force, offered under the pressure of the sexual unconscious. Drawing on multisited ethnographic research in Argentina, Brazil, and Chile, and engaging Lacan's theory of the analytic act alongside Laplanche's concept of the enigma, the article advocates a shift toward recognizing acts as eruptions of unbinding that disrupt patriarchal and colonial norms and enable new translations of the sexual unconscious. In doing so, it offers a framework for engaging the erotics of the act as a field of interruption, libidinal reconfiguration, and invention.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dreams and Eye Movement Desensitization Reprocessing

George Holliday BSc MSc

George Holliday

Northampton, United Kingdom
https://orcid.org/0009-0004-2556-3042
Email: georgeholliday@hotmail.co.uk

George Holliday  

psychoanalysis, eye movement desensitization reprocessing, trauma, dreams, psychotherapy

Interdisciplinary work between psychoanalysis and computational neuroscience has led to iterations of psychoanalytic theory (M. L. Solms, 2018). Importantly, it has bolstered orthodox psychoanalytic claims of dreams revealing unconscious beliefs, using the language of predictive processing (Koslowski et al., 2023; Nir & Tononi, 2010). Within predictive processing, dreams are equivalent to memories or hallucinations, where visual imagery is predicated upon deeper priors in “mnemonic” and “libidinal” systems. Dreams are suggested as elaborating priors—from nondeclarative forms into declarative form (Koslowski et al., 2023). Also enabling the affect to be better revealed, due to secondary defenses when awake. With a predictive processing account of eye movement desensitization reprocessing postulated by Chamberlin (2019), it is logical to suppose eye movement desensitization reprocessing could remediate distress caused by repressed priors, as revealed in dreams. This article provides grounds to explore dreams as material in eye movement desensitization reprocessing, extending its therapeutic scope.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Wireless Connection”: A Contradiction in Terms? From Three-Dimensional to Two-Dimensional: Digital Psychoanalytic Therapy

Emma Brijs MA , Dries Dulsster PHD and Stijn Vanheule PHD

Stijn Vanheule

Henri Dunantlaan 2, 9000 Ghent, Belgium
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4494-8579
Email: emma.brijs@ugent.be

Stijn Vanheule  

psychoanalysis, digital therapy, online therapy, corporeality, spatiality

This study explores the evolving landscape of psychoanalytic therapy in the digital age, focusing on the shift from traditional in-person sessions to online formats following the COVID-19 pandemic. Grounded in psychoanalytic theory, the research investigates how the transition from a three-dimensional therapeutic setting to a two-dimensional, screen-based interaction affects key elements such as corporeality, spatiality, and the integrity of the therapeutic frame. Through semistructured interviews with seven psychoanalytically oriented therapists, the study examines the impact on transference dynamics, embodied presence, and free association. The findings reveal that while online therapy offers convenience, it challenges the depth of psychoanalytic processes. Key concerns include the disruption of therapeutic rituals, the loss of embodied interaction, and the psychological distance created by the digital interface. Despite these challenges, the study identifies benefits for some patients, as the physical distance can facilitate expression for those who find in-person sessions engulfing. Ultimately, this research contributes to understanding the digitalization of psychoanalytic therapy, suggesting that while digital therapy can complement traditional approaches, it cannot fully replace the embodied experience of face-to-face sessions. These insights aim to inform future psychoanalytic practices, encouraging a balanced consideration of digital and in-person therapeutic modalities.
